text: Journal article: High-Vocabulary Speech BCI with Intracortical Microelectrodes Using a Person With ALS Abstract Abstract: Abstract Abstract Abstract Abstract Speech brain–computer interfaces (BCIs) offer promise to restore fluent communication for individuals with paralysis by translating neural activity associated with speech-related auditory evoked potentials, motor evoked potentials, or evoked potentials elicited by attempts to speak into sentences or sounds in the form of spoken language or text. Current studies and speech BCI prototypes show very limited ability to communicate general sentences or phrases from the standard language dictionaries comprising thousands to tens of thousands of words 1–7. Using a speech BCI that reads brain activity from intracortical microelectrode arrays, we report that our study participant who can no longer speak in meaning ful sentences because of amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) achieved performance of 9.1% word error rate for a 50 word vocabulary (2.7x) and 24% word error rate for a 125,000 word vocabulary—both being record-breakers to the best of our knowledge. His speech-related neural activity was translated into text at the unprecedented rate of 62 words/min (3.4x) — a rate closer to normal conversation, which is 160 words/minute. The speech-related neural activity underlying speech BCIs is even more promising. First, in contradiction to widely held assumptions of a small, spatially segregated cortical representation corresponding to speech articulators due to the small amount of cortex that has to be used for a speech BCI, we found that cortical tuning for speech articulators is mixed and spread across large distances within the cortex, which means a relatively small patch of cortex can be sufficient for a speech BCI. Second, the fine-granularity of the phoneme-related articulatory features are preserved many years after neural degeneration, which makes possible the precise motor speech rehabilitation needed to prepare participants for speech BCI operation. These results open new pathways for a possible speech BCI based communication for those with paralysis. Read the full article in Journal of Neural Engineering.
explanation: Repetitive 'Abstract' placeholders and some awkward phrasing/typos are typical artifacts of AI-generated or machine-translated text. While the technical content could be authentic, these signs increase the likelihood of AI authorship.
